# Abstract {.unnumbered}

The online gaming platform Steam allows players to post reviews indicating whether or not they recommend a game. These reviews can provide a potential buyer with more information regarding others' satisfaction and engagement. For developers, understanding the factors that influence whether a player recommends a game can help them gain insight into what their game needs to succeed. This project investigates whether player behaviour and review text can accurately predict Steam review sentiment

A dataset of 2 million English-language reviews was constructed from a publicly available collection of over 100 million Steam reviews, found on Kaggle. The dataset features a 70/30 positive to negative class split. Four classification models were developed and compared. These models include, a baseline Logistic Regression model, an extended TF-IDF Logistic Regression model, a Random Forest classifier, and a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) neural network. All models were evaluated using a combination of metrics: accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score, along with a collection of visualizations. 

The results indicate a clear structure in performance. The baseline Logistic Regression model achieved an F1-score of 0.784, while the extended TF-IDF version improved upon this to raise the F1-score to 0.875. The Random Forest model achieved a score of 0.860, and the tuned LSTM model had the best performance with a score of 0.925. These results indicate that while player behaviour is capable of producing a decent model, the strongest predictor of sentiment lies in the text contents of the review.
